questions.prompt: What does a yaw input make a drone do?
options: Rotates the drone in place around its vertical axis (the nose turns left/right)
Translates the drone sideways
Moves the drone up/down
Tilts the drone forward/backward
questions.explanation: Yaw rotates the drone around a vertical axis; its position doesn't change, only the direction the nose points. Just like spinning in place on the spot.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between roll and yaw?
options: Roll rotates the drone, yaw moves it sideways
Roll tilts/translates the drone sideways, while yaw rotates the drone in place
They are exactly the same thing
Roll controls throttle, yaw controls altitude
questions.explanation: Roll banks the drone and moves it sideways; yaw turns the nose and rotates the drone in place. Both feel like 'left-right,' but they do different things.

questions.prompt: On a standard Mode 2 radio, which movement controls yaw?
options: Up/down movement of the right stick
Left/right movement of the right stick
Left/right movement of the left stick
Up/down movement of the left stick
questions.explanation: In Mode 2, yaw is the left/right movement of the left stick. The up/down of that same left stick controls throttle.

questions.prompt: What is the most common mistake new pilots make when yawing?
options: Installing the propellers incorrectly
Connecting the battery backwards
Accidentally touching the throttle, which is on the same stick as yaw, and disturbing altitude
Forgetting to take off the goggles
questions.explanation: Because yaw and throttle are on the same (left) stick, it's very common to accidentally move the stick up/down while turning the nose, touching the throttle and losing altitude.

questions.prompt: Why do the controls get harder when the drone's nose turns toward you?
options: Because the controls feel reversed: when the drone faces you, your right is the drone's left
Because the battery drains faster
Because yaw stops working
Because the throttle shuts off automatically
questions.explanation: When the nose points at you, the drone's orientation is opposite to yours, so roll/pitch inputs feel reversed. That's why you should take small steps and practice in a simulator first.
